context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help starting this reflection tbh. I’m kinda stuck on the globalization/governance prompt and idk how to turn it into a clear argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before worrying about structure, let's think about the ideas. In your own words, what do you think 'planning capacity' means for a government? What does a government need in order to plan effectively?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs direction without me doing the work for them. I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core concepts before they write anything. I'll ask them to reflect on what they already think about the relationship between globalization and planning capacity, which is the first sub-question of the prompt. This avoids scaffolding too much while activating their prior knowledge.

exchanges.student: I think it means how much a gov can actually make plans happen, not just write them lol. So maybe money, legit authority, decent institutions, info, and staff capacity?
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — those are real dimensions of planning capacity. Now, thinking about globalization specifically: which of those elements (money, authority, institutions, information, staff) do you think globalization most disrupts or changes for a government? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid intuitive definition of planning capacity. I should affirm what's useful without over-praising, and immediately push them toward the next step: connecting that definition to globalization. This moves the Socratic dialogue forward toward the core argument they need to build.

exchanges.student: Prob authority and money most? Like capital can move fast globally, so govs maybe have less control, and cities get pushed to compete for investment fr.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation about capital mobility. But is this the whole picture? Does globalization only reduce government control, or could it also give planners something to work with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good connection between capital mobility and reduced government authority/control. This is a core insight about how globalization constrains planning capacity. I should validate the direction and push them to think more critically - specifically about whether this is uniformly true or if there are nuances (e.g., does globalization also create opportunities?). This sets up the 'reap benefits/protect against adverse effects' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Yeah true, not only reduce it. It can bring investment, new tech, policy ideas, and maybe more tax base if growth happens, but idk how to say that without sounding too generic.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real writing challenge. The way to avoid sounding generic is usually to take a clearer position on the tension you're describing. So: do you think the risks of globalization outweigh the opportunities for city planners, or the other way around? What's your actual take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is recognizing the dual nature of globalization but is worried about sounding generic. This is a good meta-awareness about writing quality. Rather than helping them with language (which I must not do), I should push them to think more concretely - asking them to think of a specific tension or trade-off that could anchor their argument and make it more pointed. This will help them develop an actual thesis rather than a list of pros and cons.

exchanges.student: I think it’s mixed, but overall city planners are more vulnerable unless they’ve got strong institutions. Like globalization creates options, but weaker cities kinda get exposed to shocks more than they control outcomes.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid position and it could anchor your whole reflection. Notice how it naturally leads into the governance questions in the prompt — if strong institutions are the key, then what does 'good governance' actually mean in this context, and who gets to define it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a clear, nuanced position that could serve as a thesis: city planners are more vulnerable to globalization without strong institutions. This is a meaningful argument that connects directly to the prompt. I should affirm this and push them toward the governance part of the prompt, since they've essentially set up the argument for why governance matters. This moves the conversation forward productively toward the second half of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess “good governance” means transparency, accountability, capacity, and being able to coordinate across levels. But I’m confused on who’s driving it—local states, donors, IFIs, maybe NGOs too?
exchanges.tutor: Good - you've identified the key actors. Now think about the implications: does it matter WHO is driving the governance agenda? What difference does it make if the push comes from the World Bank versus from local governments themselves?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a decent list of good governance components and is genuinely wrestling with the 'who is driving it' question. This is a critical question in the prompt and in development planning theory - the tension between external actors (IFIs, donors) pushing a governance agenda versus local ownership. I should push them to think about the implications of that tension rather than just listing actors, since that's where the real analytical content lies.

exchanges.student: Yeah it matters a lot imo, bc if donors/IFIs drive it, “good governance” can turn into outside conditions instead of local priorities. If local govs drive it, it prob has more legitimacy, but maybe less money/power behind it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really strong insight — you're touching on a core tension in development planning between legitimacy and power. Now, one last thing to wrestle with before you start writing: do you think governance failure causes globalization's negative effects, or is it the other way around? Or both?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent observation about the tension between external legitimacy/resources and local ownership - this directly connects to a core theme of the course (planning, power and legitimacy). This is good analytical content. I should affirm this and connect it back to the last sub-question of the prompt: is governance failure a cause or consequence of globalization? This will help them think about their conclusion. We're also about 6 exchanges in, so I should start nudging them toward synthesizing what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I think it’s both tbh. Weak governance makes places more exposed to bad global shocks, but globalization can also strain institutions and make governance failures worse.
exchanges.tutor: You've now got strong material covering basically every part of the prompt. It's time to stop discussing and start drafting. Try writing an opening paragraph that states your main argument — something built around your key insight that city planners are more vulnerable without strong institutions, and that governance and globalization reinforce each other. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all the major sub-questions of the prompt and has developed a coherent set of arguments: planning capacity depends on institutions, globalization creates both vulnerability and opportunity, good governance is contested and externally driven governance lacks legitimacy, and governance failure and globalization are mutually reinforcing. This is enough conceptual groundwork. Following the rule about integrating discussion into written content after sufficient exchanges, I should now push the student to start drafting rather than continuing to discuss.

exchanges.student: Ok, maybe: Globalization has expanded the possibilities of development planning by bringing cities investment, knowledge, and new connections, but it has also weakened governments’ ability to control economic outcomes. As a result, city planners are often more vulnerable to global market fluctuations, especially where institutions are weak and local governments lack authority, resources, or legitimacy. In this sense, globalization does not simply reduce planning capacity; it reshapes it unevenly, rewarding places with strong governance while exposing weaker ones to greater risk. This is why the governance agenda matters so much in development planning: good governance can help local planners capture the benefits of globalization and reduce its harms, but governance itself is shaped by both local political conditions and external actors.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong opening — clear argument, good nuance, and it sets up the rest of the reflection well. One thing worth asking yourself: your insight about WHO drives the governance agenda (IFIs vs. local governments and the legitimacy tension) isn't really visible yet. Where in your reflection do you plan to bring that in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a strong opening paragraph that captures their main argument clearly. It hits most of the key themes: uneven impact of globalization on planning capacity, institutional strength as the differentiating factor, and the governance agenda. I should give honest feedback without grading, note what works well, and push them to identify what still needs to be developed in the body of the reflection - particularly the 'who drives the governance agenda' tension they identified earlier, which isn't fully reflected here yet.

exchanges.student: Yeah, good point — maybe in the middle after I define good governance? I wanna connect it to how the agenda can help capacity, but also impose outside priorities, and I’m not sure how to phrase that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: Good structural instinct. But I can't help with phrasing — that part is yours to figure out. Just try drafting that middle section. Don't overthink it; write a rough version and you can refine it later. What matters now is getting your ideas down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now thinking about structure and is asking me implicitly to help with phrasing, which I must not do. I should redirect them firmly but constructively - affirm the structural instinct, remind them I can't help with language, and push them to just attempt a draft of that middle section rather than planning it further. We've had a good number of exchanges and the student should be writing now.
